article_title: I Deployed My Own Flatcar Linux Update Server and Here’s What Actually Happened
core_thesis: Deploying a self-hosted Nebraska update server gives you full control over Flatcar Linux updates in your homelab or production environment, enabling staged rollouts, testing, and rollback strategies. Nebraska is a passive Omaha-protocol server that Flatcar nodes check in with; it does not push updates or manage nodes directly. The article walks through installation via Helm on Kubernetes, configuration via Ignition files, and troubleshooting an IP address visibility issue caused by Kubernetes proxying.
detailed_summary: The author, Brandon Lee, describes his experience deploying Nebraska, the open-source update server for Flatcar Linux. Flatcar is a minimal, immutable container OS that uses dual partitions for safe, automatic updates. By default, nodes rely on the public Flatcar update service, but Nebraska allows you to control update timing, groups, and channels. Nebraska is not an agent-based management tool; it is an Omaha-protocol server that responds to node check-ins. Nodes must be configured via an Ignition file to point to the Nebraska server, and they register themselves upon first check-in. The author installed Nebraska using a Helm chart in a Kubernetes cluster, with Traefik for ingress and basic auth. He provides the full kustomization.yaml, namespace.yaml, ingressroute.yaml, and values-nebraska.yaml files. After deployment, he noticed that Nebraska displayed the Kubernetes pod IP (10.244.x.x) instead of the actual node IP (10.1.149.187). This was because traffic passed through the Ingress controller and kube-proxy, rewriting the source IP. The fix was to set externalTrafficPolicy: Local on the service to preserve the original client IP. The article concludes that Nebraska is a simple but powerful tool for managing Flatcar fleets, giving you control over update policies and rollout groups.

entities.name: Flatcar Linux

entities.name: Nebraska
entities.role: Free and open-source Omaha update server for Flatcar Linux, self-hosted via Helm chart.

entities.name: Kubernetes
entities.role: Container orchestration platform used to deploy Nebraska.

entities.role: Ingress controller used to expose Nebraska with SSL and basic auth.

entities.name: Ignition
entities.role: Configuration system used to provision Flatcar nodes, including update server URL.

key_data_points: Nebraska is a passive update server using the Omaha protocol; Flatcar nodes initiate check-ins, not the server. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: you control update timing without agent overhead.)

Nebraska does not discover or push updates; nodes must be configured via Ignition file with GROUP, SERVER, and MACHINE_ALIAS. (🟢 Important for Red Teaming: ensures no unexpected network traffic; updates are client-initiated.)

The author deployed Nebraska using Helm chart version 2.0.0 with PostgreSQL backend, Traefik ingress, and basic auth middleware. (🟢 Practical for homelab: full configuration files provided for replication.)

Nebraska showed the Kubernetes pod IP (10.244.x.x) instead of the real node IP due to source IP rewriting by kube-proxy. Fix: set externalTrafficPolicy: Local on the service. (🟢 Critical troubleshooting tip for homelab networking.)

Flatcar uses dual partitions for updates: downloads to passive partition, then switches on reboot. Nebraska only controls which version is offered. (🟢 Relevant to DevOps: safe update mechanism with rollback capability.)
The Ignition file snippet to point a node to Nebraska: GROUP=stable, SERVER=https://nebraska.example.com/v1/update/, MACHINE_ALIAS=flatcar-proxmox. (🟢 Directly actionable for homelab deployment.)
methodology_steps: 1. Deploy Nebraska using Helm: create a kustomization.yaml that references the Nebraska Helm chart (repo: https://flatcar.github.io/nebraska, version 2.0.0) and apply with `kubectl apply -k .`.

2. Configure values-nebraska.yaml: set image tag to 3.0.0, enable PostgreSQL with persistent storage (5Gi), disable ingress (use your own), set auth mode to 'noop' (or configure GitHub auth if possible), and set syncer interval to 1h.
3. Create a namespace.yaml with pod security baseline labels.

4. Create a Traefik IngressRoute (nebraska-ingressroute.yaml) with two rules: one for /v1/update/ (no auth) and one for the dashboard (with basic auth). Add middleware for forwarded headers and basic auth.
6. Configure Flatcar nodes: add a storage entry in the Butane/Ignition file to write /etc/flatcar/update.conf with GROUP, SERVER (pointing to your Nebraska /v1/update/ endpoint), and MACHINE_ALIAS.
7. Force an update check on the node: `sudo update_engine_client -update`.
8. If IP addresses appear as Kubernetes pod IPs, patch the Nebraska service to set externalTrafficPolicy: Local to preserve the original client IP.
conclusions: Nebraska is a simple, effective tool for managing Flatcar updates in a homelab or production environment. It gives you control over update groups, channels, and rollout policies without adding agent complexity. (Supports homelab need for self-hosted infrastructure control.)

The passive Omaha protocol means nodes must be pre-configured to point to Nebraska; there is no auto-discovery. This is a design choice that keeps the system lightweight and secure. (Relevant to Red Teaming: minimal attack surface.)

The IP address issue is a common Kubernetes networking pitfall. The fix (externalTrafficPolicy: Local) is straightforward and preserves accurate client IPs for monitoring. (Practical troubleshooting for homelab Kubernetes users.)

Flatcar's dual-partition update mechanism combined with Nebraska's policy engine provides a robust update workflow suitable for staging and testing updates before full rollout. (Useful for DevOps: safe updates in CI/CD pipelines.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests. It provides a complete, step-by-step guide to deploying Nebraska, a self-hosted update server for Flatcar Linux, using Helm on Kubernetes. You can directly replicate this setup to gain full control over OS updates in your homelab, enabling staged rollouts and testing. The troubleshooting tip for preserving real client IPs is a practical networking lesson. As a programmer, you can extend the configuration (e.g., integrate GitHub auth, automate node registration). For Red Teaming, understanding that updates are client-initiated and passive reduces the attack surface of your update infrastructure.
extra_advice: Consider integrating Nebraska with your existing monitoring stack (e.g., Prometheus) to track update status across your Flatcar fleet. You could also automate the Ignition file generation using a CI/CD pipeline to dynamically assign nodes to different update groups based on environment (dev/staging/prod).
